```json
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Critical SQL injection vulnerability in public-facing endpoint — trivially exploitable."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "SQL Injection via f-string query construction",
      "risk": "Attacker can read, modify, or delete entire database contents through crafted customer_id or status_filter values",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — query construction on lines 2-5",
      "reproduction": "GET /deliveries?customer_id=' OR '1'='1' --&status_filter=anything → returns all deliveries for all customers. GET /deliveries?customer_id=' UNION SELECT * FROM users --&status_filter=x → exfiltrates user table. GET /deliveries?customer_id='; DROP TABLE deliveries; --&status_filter=x → destroys data.",
      "fix": "Use parameterized queries: query = 'SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?'; result = db_conn.execute(query, (customer_id, status_filter))",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security: no user-supplied input may be interpolated into SQL"
    },
    {
      "title": "SELECT * exposes internal columns to API consumers",
      "risk": "Schema changes silently leak new columns (internal notes, cost data, PII) to the public API response; also breaks clients if columns are renamed or removed",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — SELECT *",
      "reproduction": "Add an internal_notes or margin_cost column to the deliveries table — it immediately appears in public API responses with no code change required.",
      "fix": "Enumerate columns explicitly: SELECT id, customer_id, status, tracking_number, created_at FROM deliveries",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "API contract stability and data minimization"
    },
    {
      "title": "No authorization boundary — any customer_id accepted from request",
      "risk": "Authenticated user A can query deliveries for user B by passing a different customer_id, leading to full IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — customer_id parameter sourced directly from HTTP query params",
      "reproduction": "Authenticate as user A, call GET /deliveries?customer_id=USER_B_ID&status_filter=delivered — returns user B's deliveries.",
      "fix": "Derive customer_id from the authenticated session/token, never from query parameters: customer_id = request.auth.user_id",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization: users must only access their own deliveries"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on status_filter",
      "description": "status_filter accepts any string. Should be validated against an allow-list of known statuses (e.g., pending, shipped, delivered, cancelled) to prevent unexpected queries and provide clear error messages.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — status_filter parameter"
    },
    {
      "title": "No pagination or result limit",
      "description": "A customer with thousands of deliveries (or an attacker with the SQLi) returns unbounded rows, risking OOM and denial of service. Add LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based pagination.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — query has no LIMIT clause"
    },
    {
      "title": "Raw rows returned without serialization",
      "description": "fetchall() returns driver-specific row objects. The API layer likely needs dicts or a defined schema. Returning raw rows couples the API response to the DB driver.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — return result.fetchall()"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add an index on (customer_id, status) if one does not exist — this query will be called per-request on a public API",
    "Add request-rate limiting on this endpoint to prevent enumeration attacks",
    "Log query metadata (customer_id requested vs authenticated user) for audit trail"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is actively exploitable the moment it reaches any environment with real traffic. The SQL injection requires zero authentication bypass — it is in the query parameters themselves.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe but does not undo damage from exploitation that occurs between deploy and rollback.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Fixing the SQLi and IDOR changes no documented API contract, so fixes are backward compatible.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring mentioned. At minimum, add alerting on query errors (failed injection attempts will throw SQL syntax errors) and anomalous result set sizes.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["auth_security: endpoint lacks authorization check, allowing cross-customer data access"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the commit. If already exploited, initiate incident response — audit DB logs for injected queries, assess data exposure."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 99,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["SQL injection is textbook and unambiguous", "IDOR is confirmed by the stated design (customer_id from query params)", "SELECT * data leakage risk"],
    "low_confidence_areas": [],
    "assumptions": ["db_conn.execute supports parameterized queries (true for all standard Python DB-API 2.0 drivers)", "No ORM or middleware sanitization exists upstream that we cannot see"]
  }
}
```
